claim: Somatic missense mutations in NT5C2 are associated with relapse of acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

result.claim: Somatic missense mutations in NT5C2 are associated with relapse of acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
result.reasoning: The claim is strongly supported by evidence from two papers. Paper ID 641786, "Relapse specific mutations in NT5C2 in childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia," directly states that "Transcriptome sequencing identified 20 newly acquired, novel nonsynonymous mutations not present at initial diagnosis, with 2 individuals harboring relapse-specific mutations in the same gene, NT5C2... Clinically, all individuals who harbored NT5C2 mutations relapsed early... These results suggest that mutations in NT5C2 are associated with the outgrowth of drug-resistant clones in ALL." Similarly, Paper ID 6421792, "Activating mutations in the NT5C2 nucleotidase gene drive chemotherapy resistance in relapsed ALL," identifies "mutations in the cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II gene (NT5C2)... in 20/103 (19%) relapse T cell ALLs and 1/35 (3%) relapse B-precursor ALLs." It further notes that "NT5C2 mutant proteins show increased nucleotidase activity in vitro and conferred resistance to chemotherapy... These results support a prominent role for activating mutations in NT5C2 and increased nucleoside-analog metabolism in disease progression and chemotherapy resistance in ALL." Both papers provide clear evidence linking NT5C2 mutations (nonsynonymous and activating, which include missense mutations) to relapse in acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
